skill.name: crispr-screen-analyzer
skill.description: Process CRISPR screening data to identify essential genes and hit candidates. Performs quality control, statistical analysis (RRA), and hit calling for pooled CRISPR screens including viability screens and drug resistance/sensitivity studies.
skill.summary: Analyze pooled CRISPR screening data to identify essential genes and drug response candidates using quality control, statistical analysis, and hit identification methods.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 131 static analysis findings are false positives. The 27 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' alerts (HIGH) match on documentation table keywords in SKILL.md, not actual cryptographic code. The 97 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' alerts (MEDIUM) are Markdown code block backticks, not Ruby execution. The 7 'Hardcoded URL' alerts (LOW) point to legitimate scientific references. The 4 'System reconnaissance' and 1 'Network reconnaissance' alerts (LOW) match standard Python print statements and a security checklist affirmation. The skill is a legitimate bioinformatics tool that executes Python scripts locally to process CRISPR screening data with no evidence of malicious intent.

content.user_title: Analyze CRISPR Screens to Find Essential Genes
content.value_statement: CRISPR screening experiments generate complex count data that is difficult to interpret manually. This skill processes pooled CRISPR screen data to identify essential genes and drug response candidates using quality control, statistical analysis, and hit identification.
actual_capabilities: Calculate quality control metrics including Gini index, read depth, and sgRNA dropout rates
Compute log fold changes between treatment and control conditions
Perform Robust Rank Aggregation statistical analysis with FDR correction
Identify significant hit genes using configurable FDR and fold change thresholds
Aggregate sgRNA-level results to gene-level statistics
Support multi-condition comparisons for drug resistance and sensitivity studies
limitations: Does not perform CRISPR library design or sgRNA sequence design
Cannot generate publication-ready figures or visualizations
Limited to z-score and FDR methods, does not support MAGeCK, DESeq2, or BAGEL algorithms
Requires pre-processed count matrices as input, not raw sequencing data

use_cases.title: Identify essential genes in cancer cell lines
use_cases.description: Process viability CRISPR screen data to find genes required for cancer cell survival and proliferation.

use_cases.title: Discover drug resistance mechanisms
use_cases.description: Analyze drug versus control CRISPR screens to identify genes that confer resistance or sensitivity to candidate therapeutics.

use_cases.title: Learn CRISPR screen analysis workflow
use_cases.description: Follow a guided analysis of public CRISPR screen data to understand quality control and hit calling methods.

prompt_templates.title: Basic CRISPR screen quality check
prompt_templates.prompt: I have an sgRNA count matrix and sample sheet. Help me assess the quality of my CRISPR screen using Gini index and read depth metrics.
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time user checking if their screen data is suitable for downstream analysis

prompt_templates.title: Drug resistance hit identification
prompt_templates.prompt: I completed a genome-wide CRISPR screen treating cells with Drug X versus DMSO control. Help me identify genes that confer resistance to Drug X using RRA analysis.
prompt_templates.scenario: Pharmacology researcher analyzing a drug screen with multiple replicates

prompt_templates.title: Multi-condition CRISPR comparison
prompt_templates.prompt: I performed CRISPR screens under three drug conditions plus a common control. Help me compare results across conditions to find common and condition-specific genetic dependencies.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user comparing hits across multiple treatment conditions

prompt_templates.title: Gene-level aggregation from sgRNA results
prompt_templates.prompt: I have sgRNA-level results from a CRISPR screen with gene annotations. Help me aggregate these to gene-level statistics and identify high-confidence hit genes requiring multiple sgRNAs per gene.
prompt_templates.scenario: Bioinformatics researcher refining sgRNA results to gene-level conclusions

output_examples.input: Analyze my CRISPR screen comparing Drug X treatment versus DMSO control with three replicates each.
output: Quality Control Assessment: Gini index of 0.21 indicates good library representation across all samples.
Total reads range from 12.5 million to 15.2 million per sample, above the recommended 10 million minimum threshold.
RRA Analysis: 847 sgRNAs significant at FDR below 0.05, with 342 depleted and 505 enriched.
Top hit genes include ABCB1 for resistance and RPL18A for sensitivity based on FDR and fold change thresholds.

output_examples.input: Run quality control on my new CRISPR screen count matrix with four samples.
output: QC Results: Gini indices range from 0.18 to 0.25 across all 4 samples indicating acceptable library representation.
Zero-count sgRNAs: Average 2.3 percent dropout rate, well within the normal range of below 5 percent.
Read depth: All samples exceed 10 million reads. Replicate correlation is 0.92.
Screen quality is adequate for downstream analysis. Proceed with hit identification.

best_practices: Always check Gini index first as the primary quality metric values above 0.4 indicate library bias that may invalidate downstream results
Require multiple significant sgRNAs per gene before calling a hit to reduce false positives from off-target effects
Compare results with known essential genes as positive controls to validate screen quality and statistical power
anti_patterns: Using a single sgRNA per gene as evidence for hit calling without requiring additional validation from other sgRNAs
Ignoring quality control metrics and proceeding directly to hit identification without assessing screen quality
Applying the same FDR threshold across different screen types without considering effect size and biological relevance

faq.question: What input files does this skill need?
faq.answer: You need an sgRNA count matrix in tab-separated format and a sample annotation file describing your control and treatment samples.

faq.question: What statistical method does this skill use?
faq.answer: This skill uses z-score based significance testing with Benjamini-Hochberg FDR correction for multiple testing control.

faq.question: Can this skill process raw sequencing data?
faq.answer: No. This skill requires pre-processed sgRNA count matrices. Use tools like MAGeCK or CellRanger for raw sequencing alignment and counting.

faq.question: How do I interpret the Gini index?
faq.answer: Gini index measures sgRNA library evenness. Values below 0.3 indicate good representation. Values above 0.4 suggest potential library bias.

faq.question: Does this skill support drug combination screens?
faq.answer: Yes. You can analyze any number of treatment conditions by specifying control and treatment sample groups in the configuration.

faq.question: What is the recommended FDR threshold?
faq.answer: FDR below 0.05 is standard for most screens. Use FDR below 0.01 for high-confidence hits and below 0.1 for exploratory analysis.
